description: RFC reading order. Each entry becomes an 'RFCs in Context' article with deep web research.

files.title: Internet Protocol
files.note: IPv4 — the packet format everything else rides on. Jon Postel's original design vs what we actually use.

files.title: Transmission Control Protocol
files.note: TCP — reliable delivery, sequence numbers, the state machine. The thing hop 8 owns.

files.title: User Datagram Protocol
files.note: UDP — the simplicity that QUIC exploits to bypass middleboxes.

files.title: Domain Names - Implementation and Specification
files.note: DNS — the phone book of the internet. Compression pointers, zone transfers, the works.

files.title: Hypertext Transfer Protocol -- HTTP/1.1
files.note: HTTP/1.1 — the protocol that ate the world. Connection management, chunked encoding, the upgrade mechanism.

files.title: The WebSocket Protocol
files.note: WebSocket — persistent bidirectional over HTTP Upgrade. The protocol being killed at hop 8.

files.title: QUIC: A UDP-Based Multiplexed and Secure Transport
files.note: QUIC — encrypted transport that middleboxes can't touch. Why it succeeds where TCP fails.

files.title: The Transport Layer Security (TLS) Protocol Version 1.3
files.note: TLS 1.3 — the encryption layer. ClientHello, SNI, and why transparent proxies can still see where you're going.

files.title: A Border Gateway Protocol 4 (BGP-4)
files.note: BGP — how the internet routes. AS boundaries, peering, and why hop 8 sits where it does.

files.title: Internet Control Message Protocol
files.note: ICMP — ping, traceroute, and the messages routers aren't required to send. Why hop 8 stays silent.

files.title: An Ethernet Address Resolution Protocol
files.note: ARP — layer 2 to layer 3 resolution. The thing you poison when you're on the same segment.

files.title: Internet Protocol, Version 6
files.note: IPv6 — the future that's been arriving for 25 years. Extension headers, flow labels, and why transition is hard.
